_description: Question banks for /ingest interview sub-phases. Loaded by ingest-protocol.md. Covers all 7 sub-phases from source identity through workshop synthesis. Structure: array of question objects with phase, question_id, question_text, expected_answer_shape, follow_up_triggers.

questions.question_text: Which sources are we ingesting today? List them — file name or paste label is fine.
questions.expected_answer_shape: list of source identifiers (file names, paste labels, or descriptions)

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: That is a large set. Which three are the most important to extract from — the ones where the org's voice and channel conventions are clearest?

questions.question_text: For each source, who was the intended speaker and which audience was it written for? (E.g., 'This email was from the CHRO to all employees' or 'This PPTX was the board deck.')
questions.expected_answer_shape: per-source: speaker slug + audience slug + cycle year

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: If the speaker is unknown, what is your best guess from the tone or signature? We can mark it as 'inferred' in the profile.

questions.question_text: Is anything in this source set still in draft or not final? Any source we should NOT extract from yet?
Looking at the {speaker} materials: how would you describe the tone in three words? (Not what you want the tone to be — what it actually is in these sources.)

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: Those are defaults. Let's get more specific: is it warm-formal or cold-formal? Does this speaker use 'we' a lot, or 'I'? Do they lead with emotion or facts?

questions.question_text: What words or phrases does {speaker} use repeatedly that are distinctly theirs — things you would NOT expect from another speaker?
questions.expected_answer_shape: list of 3-8 distinctive phrases or words

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: Look at the opening sentence of each {speaker} email or memo. What pattern do you see? Do they start with 'I want to share', 'As we discussed', 'Today marks'?

questions.question_text: What phrases or words has {speaker} explicitly rejected or been asked to remove in past review rounds?
questions.expected_answer_shape: list of flagged phrases + reason for rejection

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: Think about the last time a draft came back with edits. What was the first thing that changed? That is usually the clearest signal about what the speaker dislikes.

questions.question_text: How does {speaker} typically sign off? Do they use a formal sign-off phrase, or do they end with a CTA?
What does the signature block look like — name, title, contact? Is there a convention (e.g., phone number included or excluded)?
Can you paste or quote one paragraph from {speaker}'s best prior communication — the one where you said 'yes, this sounds like them'?
questions.expected_answer_shape: verbatim paragraph (will become sample_paragraph in the register)

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: Take the first paragraph of the all-hands email we reviewed. Read it out loud. Does it sound like {speaker}? If not, what word would they change first?

questions.question_text: How long does {speaker} typically write? Does the CHRO (or whoever drafts for them) impose a length limit?
questions.expected_answer_shape: word count or page count norm

questions.question_text: Is there a 'reviewer' who reads {speaker}'s drafts before they go out — a chief of staff, executive assistant, or comms lead? If so, what does that reviewer typically push back on?

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: So the real voice authority is that reviewer, not {speaker} themselves? Let's adjust the register to reflect what passes review, not what {speaker} drafts.

questions.question_text: On a scale from 1-5: how much does {speaker}'s written voice differ from their spoken voice? (1 = identical; 5 = completely different — the emails sound like a lawyer wrote them.)

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: High gap. That means the drafts are coming from someone else. Should we try to close that gap in v1 comms, or maintain the formal register because that is what the org expects from this role?

questions.question_text: Is there a specific phrase or structure {speaker} always uses to introduce hard news (e.g., wage freezes, structural changes)? And one they use to introduce good news?
For the {audience} audience: what were the top 3 questions or complaints you received after the last compensation communication?

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: Was that a confusion about the formula, or did they not trust the number they received? The fix is different depending on which it is.

questions.question_text: How does the {audience} audience typically receive compensation comms — email, intranet post, physical posting, team meeting, or some combination?
What does the {audience} audience MOST want to know when they receive compensation news — the amount, the rationale, or what they need to do?
questions.expected_answer_shape: priority ranking of: amount / rationale / action

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: What rationale resonates with this audience — market positioning, cost discipline, fairness, legal obligation? Knowing which argument lands changes how we frame the memo.

questions.question_text: On the reading-level spectrum (grade 7 / grade 9 / grade 12 / college): where does the {audience} audience sit? Think about the emails they send to HR, not about their job description.
For the {audience} audience: what topic is out of bounds in an all-hands comms? (E.g., individual salary amounts, union negotiations, specific grievances.)
Is there a segment of the {audience} audience that is particularly sensitive this cycle — recent hire cohort, employees on leave, employees nearing retirement, employees in a restructured department?
Has this audience segment ever pushed back publicly (town hall question, union grievance, social media post) on prior compensation communications? If yes, what triggered it?

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: That trigger should become an anti-pattern entry. What was the exact phrase or claim that caused it?

questions.question_text: For the {channel} channel: what is the typical length of compensation communications you have sent through it? (Word count or page count — rough is fine.)
For the {channel} channel: are there formatting conventions the org always follows — specific headings, bullet structures, callout boxes, footer disclaimers?
For email: what does the sender display line look like — first name only, full name + title, or something else? Is there a shared alias used?
For DOCX distribution: does the org use a cover page? Is there a confidentiality footer convention?
For the exec PPTX: how many slides do board-bound compensation decks typically run? Is there a hard cap from the board secretary?
Think about the last compensation comms cycle. Was there a phrase in the announcement that employees pushed back on — in team meetings, by email, or through the union?

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: What should the phrase have said instead? I'll add both to the anti-patterns list.

questions.question_text: Are there any phrases or terms you have been told NEVER to use by your legal, union relations, or comms team?
questions.expected_answer_shape: list of banned phrases + reason for each

questions.question_text: Are there FR-CA terms that have caused problems — either because they were incorrectly translated from English or because they carry a different connotation in Quebec?
questions.expected_answer_shape: list of problematic FR-CA terms + correct alternative

questions.question_text: Has there ever been a compensation communication where the announcement of good news still generated negative feedback? What do you think caused that?
questions.expected_answer_shape: narrative description of the incident + hypothesized cause

follow_up_triggers.condition: hypothesized cause is a phrase or framing
follow_up_triggers.follow_up: That framing pattern is the anti-pattern. Can you name the specific phrase that landed wrong?

questions.question_text: Are there topics that should NEVER appear in an all-hands compensation email — even if they are technically related (e.g., budget cuts, headcount changes, performance ratings)?
Does the org have a defined primary brand color? What is the hex code, or can you point me to the brand guidelines document?
questions.expected_answer_shape: hex code or brand guidelines reference

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: Paste the brand color name (e.g., 'Acme Red'). I can approximate from common brand references; we'll confirm before final render.

questions.question_text: Is there a secondary brand color used for accents or callout boxes in internal documents?
What font does the org use for internal documents — Word docs, employee emails, intranet posts? Is there a different font for headings vs. body text?
questions.expected_answer_shape: font name for headings + font name for body

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: Is the font available as a system font on standard Windows installations? If not, we will need a fallback (Calibri or Arial) for portability.

questions.question_text: Is there a standard email header banner (image) used in all-hands emails? Can you share the image file or a link to where it is stored?
questions.expected_answer_shape: file path or URL to header banner image

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: We will use the bundled placeholder header. You can update it later via /brand init after we have the image.

questions.question_text: For the CEO and CHRO email signatures: is there a standard format the org uses (text only, with logo, with title line, with contact phone)? Can you paste an example?
questions.expected_answer_shape: signature block example with format description

questions.question_text: For board-bound PPTX decks: is there an existing PPTX master or template the compensation team uses? If yes, can you share it?
questions.expected_answer_shape: yes/no + file path or Drive link if yes

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: We will use that master as the base. The comp-comms-builder exec-one-pager will follow that master's layout rather than the bundled default.

questions.question_text: Is there a logo file (PNG or SVG) available for the PDF header and PPTX title slide? Where is it stored?
Are there any brand rules that restrict how the logo or colors are used — e.g., logo must not appear on a dark background, specific clearance around the logo, minimum size?
I have drafted the org-comms-profile based on what we covered. Let me walk you through the speaker register for each speaker — does each description match what you see in the actual communications?
questions.expected_answer_shape: confirmation per speaker OR correction with specific detail

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: Noted. I will update {speaker}'s register with that change. Anything else for this speaker before we move on?

questions.question_text: For the audience profiles: do the key concerns I have listed match what you actually hear from each audience when compensation news lands?
For the anti-patterns list: are there any entries you want to remove (too org-specific to be reliable) or add before we lock the profile?
Is there anything in this profile that should NOT be shared with external parties — e.g., an anti-pattern that references a specific incident you'd rather keep internal?
questions.expected_answer_shape: list of fields to mark sensitive OR 'nothing to flag'

questions.question_text: Ready to write the org-comms-profile to Drive? This will create or update org-comms-profiles/<org-slug>.yaml. Confirm: yes / review-first / abort.

follow_up_triggers.follow_up: I will display the full profile in chat. Let me know which fields to change before we write.
Profile not written. All extracted data remains in this session. You can resume with /checkpoint to save the session state.
